The practical angle is knowing when AsyncPipe, takeUntilDestroyed, or manual teardown actually matters, when HTTP completes on its own, and how never-ending resolver streams can block navigation.

blocks.text:
Teardown mental model

Most Angular memory leaks are not mysterious framework bugs. They come from long-lived streams, timers, or global listeners that outlive a component. The production question is not “unsubscribe from everything?” but “which sources complete on their own, and which ones keep consuming memory, CPU, or network until I tear them down?”

rows: HTTP observables complete after one response.
They are long-lived streams tied to app lifetime.
They never complete unless you stop them.
blocks.caption: Which subscriptions leak if you forget to clean them up?

blocks.text:
Pattern #1: Use the async pipe (best default)

The async pipe automatically subscribes and unsubscribes when the component is destroyed. It also integrates perfectly with OnPush change detection.

Pattern #2: takeUntilDestroyed (modern Angular, recommended)

This is the cleanest way to auto-unsubscribe in code in Angular 16+ using DestroyRef.

Outside an injection context

takeUntilDestroyed() is still usable when the subscription setup happens in a helper or service method, but then you must pass an explicit DestroyRef. That detail matters when you refactor teardown logic out of the component body.
Pattern #3: Classic takeUntil + Subject (works in all Angular versions)

Pattern #4: Manual Subscription aggregation (old-school)

Works, but easy to forget to add new subscriptions. Not recommended for large components.

Non-RxJS leaks: event listeners & timers

Leaks don’t only come from Observables. addEventListener, setInterval, and 3rd-party libraries must also be cleaned up in ngOnDestroy.

Route-level pitfall: resolver streams that never complete

A resolver is not just another subscription site. If the resolver returns a stream that never completes, navigation can hang forever because the router is still waiting for resolution. That is a different failure mode from a component leak, but it comes from the same teardown misunderstanding.

rows: Older Angular or shared base classes
Easy to forget and cause leaks.
blocks.caption: Which unsubscribe strategy should you pick?

blocks.text:
Senior pitfalls

• Assuming Angular auto-unsubscribes everything (it doesn’t)
• Forgetting that ActivatedRoute, valueChanges, Subjects never complete
• Subscribing in services that live forever (root scope)
• Returning never-ending resolver streams and blocking navigation
• Leaking intervals, event listeners, and WebSocket connections

Interview summary

To prevent memory leaks in Angular: prefer the async pipe in templates, use takeUntilDestroyed (or takeUntil + Subject) in code, know which Observables complete automatically (HTTP) and which do not, and clean up timers, event listeners, and route-level long-lived streams deliberately. The hidden follow-up is that resolvers must usually complete, not just unsubscribe eventually.

seo.title: Angular memory leaks in production: long-lived streams, AsyncPipe, and teardown patterns
seo.description: Explain Angular memory leaks through real teardown bugs: long-lived subscriptions, global listeners, timers, AsyncPipe, takeUntilDestroyed, and when manual unsubscribe still matters.

incidentCard.title: Leaking Subscription Check
incidentCard.scenario: A dashboard component subscribes to `route.params`, `form.valueChanges`, and a `setInterval` poller inside `ngOnInit`. After navigating away a few times, the old component keeps logging, polling, and reacting to form changes. One teammate says, "Angular destroys components, so subscriptions and timers should disappear automatically." Another says, "This is a real teardown bug. HTTP calls usually complete on their own, but long-lived streams and timers need cleanup with patterns like `async` pipe, `takeUntilDestroyed`, or explicit `ngOnDestroy` teardown." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The first explanation is better. Once Angular removes the component from the DOM, subscriptions and intervals tied to that component should be garbage-collected without any extra teardown code.
The safest rule is to manually unsubscribe every Observable, including one-off `HttpClient` calls, because Angular and RxJS do not reliably distinguish short-lived and long-lived streams.

The second explanation is better. Component destruction does not magically unsubscribe every long-lived stream or stop timers. `route.params`, `valueChanges`, intervals, event listeners, and similar sources need cleanup unless Angular is managing them for you, such as with the `async` pipe.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The important distinction is whether the source completes on its own. One-off HTTP calls usually complete, but long-lived streams like `valueChanges`, `route.params`, intervals, WebSockets, and global listeners keep running until you tear them down.

title: What Is ControlValueAccessor in Angular and Why Is It Better Than Custom Two-Way Binding?
description: ControlValueAccessor is the bridge that makes a custom Angular component behave like a real form control. The useful comparison is not just value syncing, but validation, touched or dirty state, disabled propagation, and the production pitfalls of relying on custom two-way binding instead.

blocks.text:
Forms-integration question

ControlValueAccessor is not just another way to sync a value. It is the contract that lets Angular Forms treat your custom component like a real form control. That is the production difference: validation, touched/dirty state, reset behavior, and disabled handling work correctly instead of being rebuilt with fragile custom two-way bindings.

Why not just use custom two-way binding?

Two-way binding like [(value)] only synchronizes a value. It does not integrate with Angular Forms, so you lose validation, touched/dirty states, disabled state, and form-level APIs. CVA plugs your component into the entire forms ecosystem.

blocks.caption: Two-way binding vs ControlValueAccessor

blocks.text:
Minimal CVA implementation

A CVA has four responsibilities:
• writeValue: Angular → component
• registerOnChange: component → Angular
• registerOnTouched: mark as touched
• setDisabledState: handle disabled
Using it like a real form control

blocks.code:
<form [formGroup]="form">
  <app-rating formControlName="rating"></app-rating>
</form>

blocks.text:
Why this is strictly better than custom two-way binding

With CVA, your component becomes a first-class Angular form control. It participates in validation, form submission, reset, disabled state propagation, and form-level state tracking. Two-way binding only moves a value around—it does not integrate into the forms system.

rows: ❌ You must wire every input manually
blocks.caption: Why CVA scales in real apps

blocks.text:
Senior-level pitfalls

• Forgetting to call onTouched() (control never becomes touched)
• Not implementing setDisabledState (disabled forms don’t work)
• Mutating internal state without calling onChange
• Using custom two-way binding for components that should be form controls

Interview summary

ControlValueAccessor is Angular’s official way to make a custom component behave like a real form control. It integrates with Reactive Forms and ngModel, supports validation, touched/dirty state, disabled handling, and form APIs. Custom two-way binding only syncs a value and does not integrate with Angular Forms, which makes it unsuitable for serious form components.

seo.title: Angular ControlValueAccessor vs custom two-way binding: real forms integration and pitfalls
seo.description: Explain ControlValueAccessor through real form behavior: validation, touched or dirty state, disabled propagation, and why custom two-way binding breaks down in serious Angular forms.

incidentCard.title: Custom Input Integration Check
incidentCard.scenario: A team built a reusable `<app-rating>` component using custom `[(value)]` binding. It updates the number on screen, but when they plug it into a reactive form, validators do not run, `touched` never updates, and disabling the form does not disable the widget. One teammate says, "Two-way binding already solves this; Angular Forms is being picky." Another says, "This component should be implemented as a ControlValueAccessor so Angular Forms can treat it like a real form control." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. Custom two-way binding only moves a value around, but ControlValueAccessor connects the component to Angular Forms so validation, touched or dirty state, reset behavior, and disabled handling all work through the normal form APIs.
The first explanation is better. Once a component supports `[(value)]`, Angular should be able to infer validation and disabled behavior automatically without needing a separate forms interface.

The better fix is to keep custom two-way binding and just add extra outputs like `blur`, `invalid`, and `disabledChange`. That gives the parent enough hooks to match Angular Forms behavior without using ControlValueAccessor.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key distinction is value syncing versus forms integration. `[(value)]` can sync state, but CVA is what makes a custom component participate correctly in Angular Forms features like validators, touched state, reset, and disabled propagation.

title: Template-Driven vs Reactive Forms in Angular: Which One Scales and Why?
description: Template-driven forms rely on directives in the template and implicit state, while reactive forms define the entire form model in TypeScript. Reactive forms scale better because they are explicit, testable, composable, and predictable for complex validation and dynamic form structures.

blocks.text:
Core idea

Both approaches create the same thing in the end (a FormGroup tree). The difference is where the form model lives:

• Template-driven: the form is inferred from the template.
• Reactive: the form is declared explicitly in TypeScript.

The more complex the form becomes, the more valuable the explicit model of reactive forms becomes.

rows: In the template (via directives like ngModel)
blocks.caption: Template-driven vs Reactive forms

blocks.text: Template-driven example (simple, but limited)

blocks.code:
<form #f="ngForm">
  <input name="email" ngModel required email />
  <button [disabled]="f.invalid">Save</button>
</form>

blocks.text: Reactive example (explicit and scalable)

blocks.code:
<form [formGroup]="form">
  <input formControlName="email" />
  <button [disabled]="form.invalid">Save</button>
</form>

blocks.text:
Why Reactive Forms scale better

In real applications you eventually need:
• Dynamic fields (add/remove rows)
• Cross-field validation
• Conditional enable/disable logic
• Programmatic resets, patches, partial updates
• Testable validation logic

All of these are natural in reactive forms and awkward in template-driven forms.

blocks.caption: Why reactive forms dominate in large apps

blocks.text:
Performance and architecture considerations

Reactive forms fit naturally with:
• OnPush change detection
• Immutable update patterns
• Observable-based workflows
• CVA-based custom controls

Template-driven forms rely heavily on directives and two-way binding, which becomes harder to control and reason about at scale.

When template-driven forms are still OK

• Very small forms (login, newsletter signup)
• Prototypes, admin panels, quick internal tools
• When you want minimal boilerplate and don’t need complex logic

Senior-level pitfalls

• Mixing template-driven and reactive approaches in the same form
• Using template-driven forms for dynamic or complex workflows
• Putting business logic in templates instead of validators/services

Interview summary

Template-driven forms are fine for simple cases, but reactive forms scale better because the form model is explicit, testable, composable, and predictable. Any serious Angular application with dynamic fields, complex validation, or non-trivial workflows should default to reactive forms.

seo.title: Template-driven vs reactive forms in Angular: which to use?
seo.description: Reactive forms scale better for complex, dynamic, and testable form logic, while template-driven forms fit simpler scenarios.

incidentCard.title: Form Strategy Check
incidentCard.scenario: A simple signup form grew into a large workflow with dynamic address rows, cross-field validation, conditional enable or disable rules, and unit tests around form logic. The team still uses template-driven bindings because it started small. One teammate says, "Just keep adding directives in the template. Template-driven forms and reactive forms are basically the same at any size." Another says, "This has crossed into reactive-forms territory because the form model needs to be explicit, testable, and easier to change in code." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The best approach is to mix both patterns in the same form. Use template-driven pieces for simple inputs and reactive controls only for the complex parts so you get the strengths of both without a full rewrite.

The second explanation is better. Once forms become dynamic, validation-heavy, and full of conditional logic, reactive forms are usually the better fit because the model lives in TypeScript, making the behavior more explicit, testable, and maintainable.
The first explanation is better. Template-driven forms scale just as well as reactive forms as long as the team keeps the template tidy and adds the right directives for each new rule.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key lesson is not that template-driven forms are bad, but that reactive forms scale better once the form stops being simple. Dynamic controls, cross-field validation, and testable business rules are where an explicit TypeScript form model pays off.

title: What Is shareReplay in RxJS and How Can It Silently Break Your Angular App?
description: shareReplay is an RxJS operator that turns a cold observable into a shared hot one and caches the last emitted values. Used incorrectly, it can cause memory leaks, stale data bugs, and unexpected behavior—especially in Angular services and HTTP streams.

blocks.text:
Core idea

shareReplay does two things at once:
• It shares a single subscription among multiple subscribers.
• It replays the last N emitted values to new subscribers.

This is extremely useful for caching HTTP calls—but also extremely easy to misuse.

The classic Angular use-case

You want multiple components to consume the same HTTP data without triggering multiple requests.

What problem does this solve?

Without shareReplay, every subscribe() or async pipe would trigger a new HTTP request. With shareReplay(1), the first request is cached and reused.

blocks.caption: Why people use shareReplay

blocks.text: How shareReplay can silently break your app

rows: By default, shareReplay keeps the source subscribed forever.
Services never released, WebSocket connections and intervals never stop.
Cached value is reused even when data should refresh.
User navigates away/back and sees outdated data.
If the source errors, the error is also replayed.
Stream is permanently broken until reload.
shareReplay turns cold streams into hot singletons.
blocks.caption: The hidden dangers

blocks.text:
The most important option: refCount

Modern RxJS lets you write:

What does refCount: true do?

It tells RxJS: “When there are no subscribers left, unsubscribe from the source and free resources.” Without this, the source stays alive forever.

blocks.caption: Why refCount matters

blocks.text:
Another subtle trap: error caching

If the HTTP call errors once, shareReplay will replay that error forever. All future subscribers instantly get the error, and the stream never retries unless you rebuild it or add retry logic.

Senior rule of thumb

• Use shareReplay for caching expensive or shared streams.
• Prefer { bufferSize: 1, refCount: true } unless you intentionally want a forever-alive cache.
• Be explicit about refresh/invalidation semantics.
• Never blindly slap shareReplay(1) everywhere.

rows: Config loaded once at app startup
shareReplay(1) without refCount may be OK
Use refCount or explicit refresh trigger
You probably want manual lifecycle control
HTTP list used by many components
blocks.caption: When to use shareReplay in Angular

blocks.text:
Interview summary

shareReplay shares a single subscription and caches the last values. It is great for avoiding duplicate HTTP calls, but can cause memory leaks, stale data, and permanent error states if used carelessly. In Angular, the safe default is shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true }) and a clear cache invalidation strategy.

seo.title: What does shareReplay do in RxJS and why break Angular apps?
seo.description: shareReplay caches and multicasts source emissions, but wrong buffer or refCount settings can retain stale data or leak subscriptions.

incidentCard.title: Sticky Stream Check
incidentCard.scenario: A service wraps a polling stream in `shareReplay(1)` so multiple components can reuse the same data. Later the team notices that after every component leaves the screen, the polling keeps running in the background and stale data sometimes shows up when a user comes back. One teammate says, "That is just how RxJS caching works, so this is the right tradeoff." Another says, "This is the classic `shareReplay` trap. On long-lived streams, `shareReplay(1)` can keep the source alive and replay outdated values unless you use something like `refCount: true` or define explicit refresh semantics." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. `shareReplay` is useful, but on long-lived streams it can keep the source subscribed and keep replaying cached data longer than you intended. In Angular, the safer default is usually `shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true })` plus an explicit refresh or invalidation strategy.

The first explanation is better. If a stream is shared across components, `shareReplay(1)` is almost always the correct default, and background activity or stale values usually point to unrelated bugs elsewhere in the app.
The main fix is to set a larger replay buffer so newer subscribers get a more complete history. The real problem is usually missing past values, not that the source stays alive too long.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The important lesson is that `shareReplay` is not just a cache; it also changes subscription behavior. That is why `shareReplay(1)` can quietly create leaks or stale-data bugs when used on long-lived streams without `refCount` or explicit invalidation.

title: What Actually Cancels an HTTP Request in Angular?
description: In Angular, HTTP requests are canceled by unsubscribing from the Observable, usually via switchMap, takeUntil, async pipe, or manual unsubscribe. The production trap is that many patterns only ignore old responses instead of truly aborting the request.

blocks.text:
Core idea

An Angular HttpClient request is just an Observable wrapper around a browser request. The only thing that can actually cancel it is unsubscribing from that Observable. When you unsubscribe, Angular tells the browser to abort the underlying request (via AbortController or XHR abort).

columns: Does it cancel the network request?
rows: Subscription is torn down → request aborted.
Old requests keep running in parallel.
Network still continues; you just drop the response.
blocks.caption: What truly cancels HTTP in Angular?

blocks.text:
The classic search example

When users type quickly, you want to cancel old requests and only keep the latest one.

Why this cancels old requests

switchMap unsubscribes from the previous inner Observable when a new value arrives. Since HttpClient cancels requests on unsubscribe, the browser request is aborted.
takeUntil pattern (component lifecycle)

Manual teardown also cancels

switchMap is not the only cancel mechanism. If you keep the subscription and call unsubscribe() yourself, Angular aborts the underlying request just the same. That matters in imperative flows like dialogs, temporary polling, or explicit “cancel” buttons.

Ignoring a result is not cancellation

If code sets a flag like ignoreOldResponses = true but keeps the old subscription alive, the browser request still runs. You only changed what your callback does with the response.

What does NOT cancel the request

Many devs think these cancel HTTP, but they don’t:

rows: “I used mergeMap, so old requests are gone”
❌ mergeMap runs everything in parallel. Nothing is canceled.
❌ Unless you unsubscribed (async pipe or takeUntil), request keeps running.
❌ The server may continue processing even if client aborts.
blocks.caption: Common misconceptions

blocks.text:
Important nuance: canceling the browser request vs canceling server work

When Angular aborts the request, the browser connection is closed. The server may or may not stop processing—this depends entirely on server implementation. From the frontend point of view, however, the request is gone.

rows: Unsubscribe is triggered by notifier or lifecycle.
May keep the request alive if refCount is false.
blocks.caption: Cancellation behavior by pattern

blocks.text:
Same flow, different operator, different cancellation behavior

These two snippets look similar, but only one actually aborts old requests when a new query arrives:

Senior-level pitfall

Using shareReplay without refCount can keep an HTTP request (or its subscription) alive forever, making it impossible to cancel later even if components unsubscribe.

Interview summary

In Angular, only unsubscribing cancels an HTTP request. Operators like switchMap, takeUntil, and the async pipe cancel requests because they unsubscribe. Operators like mergeMap do not cancel anything—they just ignore or parallelize results. Real cancellation = unsubscribe.

seo.title: Angular HttpClient cancellation in production: unsubscribe, switchMap, and false-cancel myths
seo.description: Explain Angular HTTP cancellation through real patterns: unsubscribe, switchMap, takeUntil, AsyncPipe cleanup, and why ignoring a result is not the same as aborting a request.

incidentCard.title: Request Cancellation Check
incidentCard.scenario: A search box sends a new HttpClient request on every keystroke. Users type quickly, and older responses sometimes arrive after newer ones. A teammate replaces `switchMap` with `mergeMap` and says, "It is fine, we can just ignore old responses in the subscribe block. That cancels the old request logically." Another teammate says, "Ignoring a result is not the same as canceling the request. In Angular, the request is actually canceled only when the previous HttpClient Observable is unsubscribed, which is why `switchMap` works here." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The first explanation is better. Once the UI decides not to use an older response, the browser and Angular treat that request as effectively canceled even if the network call finishes in the background.

The main rule is that Angular automatically cancels any previous HttpClient request to the same endpoint when a new one starts, so operator choice mostly affects only how results are mapped back into the UI.

The second explanation is better. In Angular, real cancellation happens when the HttpClient Observable is unsubscribed. `switchMap`, `takeUntil`, and AsyncPipe cleanup can do that. `mergeMap` does not cancel old requests; it just lets them keep running.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The core lesson is simple: real HTTP cancellation in Angular comes from unsubscribe. If nothing unsubscribes from the HttpClient Observable, the request keeps going even if your code stops caring about the result.

title: Explain Hierarchical Dependency Injection in Angular With a Real Bug Example
description: Angular DI is hierarchical, so provider location changes instance count and service lifetime. The useful angle is the real production bug: re-providing a service in a component or standalone route and silently splitting shared state, caches, or coordination logic.

seo.title: Angular hierarchical DI in production: duplicate providers, shadowed singletons, and cache bugs
seo.description: Explain Angular hierarchical DI through the real bug: a nearer provider shadows the root injector, creates a second instance, and breaks shared state or cache assumptions.

blocks.text:
Bug-first mental model

Angular DI is not a single global container. It is a tree of injectors, and the nearest provider wins. That becomes a production bug when a root service is accidentally re-provided in a component or route and your “singleton” cache, auth flag, or shared state quietly splits into multiple instances.

The real-world bug

“Why does my shared state reset when I navigate?” or “Why does my cache disappear in some components but not others?” The usual answer: you accidentally created multiple instances of the same service.

rows: In a lazy-loaded route or module
blocks.caption: Where you provide a service determines its lifetime and scope

blocks.text:
The bug in code

You think you have one cache service, but you accidentally created many.

What goes wrong

Other parts of the app inject the root instance of UserCacheService, but UserListComponent gets its own private copy. Now state, caches, and flags are silently out of sync.

How Angular resolves the dependency

1) Check the component’s injector
2) If not found, check parent component injectors
3) Then module/route injectors
4) Finally root injector

The first match wins.

blocks.caption: How the injector tree affects instance sharing

blocks.text:
Another common real bug: lazy-loaded modules

If you provide a service in a lazy-loaded route/module, you get a separate instance from the rest of the app. This often breaks auth state, caches, or WebSocket connections.

Standalone route providers are their own injector boundary

In modern Angular, route-level providers create an EnvironmentInjector for that branch. That is why a service can be a singleton at the app root but still get a separate instance under one lazy route tree.

When component-level providers are actually correct

• Reusable UI widgets with isolated state
• Temporary, per-instance state machines
• Editable rows, dialogs, or dynamic components that must not share state

blocks.caption: Correct scoping strategy

blocks.text:
Senior-level pitfalls

• Accidentally re-providing a root service in a component
• Breaking caches or auth state via lazy-loaded module or route providers
• Forgetting that standalone route providers create a new injector boundary
• Assuming “service = singleton” without checking injector scope
• Debugging “state randomly resets” for hours

Interview summary

Angular DI is hierarchical: services are resolved by walking up the injector tree. Where you provide a service determines how many instances exist. Many real-world bugs come from accidentally creating multiple instances by providing a service in a component or lazy-loaded route when you expected a singleton. In standalone Angular, route providers are especially important because they create an EnvironmentInjector for that branch.

incidentCard.title: Duplicate Service Instance Check
incidentCard.scenario: A team has `UserCacheService` marked with `providedIn: 'root'`, but one feature component also adds `providers: [UserCacheService]`. Later they notice that cache state updates in most of the app, but inside that feature the data looks like a different copy and resets unexpectedly. One teammate says, "That cannot be DI-related. A service is a singleton once it is in root." Another says, "This is exactly a hierarchical DI bug. The component-level provider is shadowing the root provider and creating a separate instance for that branch." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The first explanation is better. Once a service uses `providedIn: 'root'`, Angular always reuses that single instance everywhere, even if the same service is listed again in component or route providers.
The real issue is that child components do not inherit service instances from parent injectors, so each component naturally ends up with its own copy unless the service is passed in manually.

The second explanation is better. Angular resolves services from the nearest injector upward, so a component-level provider can override the root provider for that subtree and create a separate instance, which is why shared state quietly falls out of sync.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key lesson is that Angular DI is hierarchical, not globally flat. Where a service is provided determines its scope, and re-providing a root service in a component or lazy-loaded branch can create a second instance.

title: Subject vs BehaviorSubject vs ReplaySubject vs AsyncSubject in Angular: when do you use which?
description: All four are RxJS Subject variants, but they differ in what late subscribers receive and when values are emitted. Interviewers expect you to choose the right one based on initial value needs, replay history, completion behavior, and memory tradeoffs.

blocks.text:
Core idea

These are all multicasting primitives, but they answer different product questions:
• Do I need an initial value right now?
• Should late subscribers see old values?
• Do I only care about the final value after completion?

Pick the wrong one and your UI will either miss state, replay too much history, or never emit when you expect.

rows: Current UI state (selected tab, auth status, filter value)
Final value only, and only after completion
One-shot workflows where only the final result matters
blocks.caption: The interview-grade difference in one table

rows: No initial or replayed value needed
New subscribers need immediate current value
Keep a short in-memory event history
blocks.caption: Fast decision guide for real Angular code

rows: Using BehaviorSubject with a fake default like null
Consumers must handle meaningless initial states everywhere
Use ReplaySubject(1) if no true initial value exists
Using large ReplaySubject buffers in long-lived services
Memory grows and old state lingers too long
No value is emitted because completion never happens
Any consumer can call next() and break state discipline
Keep Subject private and expose only asObservable()
blocks.caption: Mistakes that show up in production code reviews

blocks.text:
Interview summary

Subject = live events only. BehaviorSubject = current value + future updates. ReplaySubject = recent history + future updates. AsyncSubject = only final value on completion. In Angular, default to BehaviorSubject for local UI state, Subject for intent events, and ReplaySubject only when you truly need history.

seo.title: Subject vs BehaviorSubject vs ReplaySubject vs AsyncSubject?
seo.description: Use Subject for events, BehaviorSubject for current state, ReplaySubject for buffered history, and AsyncSubject for final-on-complete emission.

incidentCard.title: Late Subscriber State Check
incidentCard.scenario: A new widget subscribes to the current filter stream after the page has already loaded. With a plain `Subject`, it renders empty until the user changes the filter again. One teammate says, "That is normal, just trigger another `.next()` when the widget mounts." Another says, "This stream represents current UI state, so late subscribers should immediately get the latest value. That is a better fit for `BehaviorSubject` or possibly `ReplaySubject(1)` if there is no real initial value." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The first explanation is better. `Subject` is the most flexible default for UI state, and the clean solution is simply to re-emit the current value whenever a new component subscribes.
The better fix is `AsyncSubject`, because the filter should only be emitted once the app has fully settled, which avoids intermediate states reaching late subscribers.

The second explanation is better. A plain `Subject` only delivers future emissions, so it is weak for "current state" streams. If late subscribers need the latest value right away, `BehaviorSubject` is the common choice, or `ReplaySubject(1)` if there is no honest initial value.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key distinction is what late subscribers should receive. `Subject` gives only future emissions, `BehaviorSubject` gives the latest current value, `ReplaySubject` gives buffered history, and `AsyncSubject` emits only the final value after completion.

title: RxJS tap vs map in Angular: side effects vs transformation (with real examples)
description: tap and map are frequently confused in Angular RxJS pipelines. map transforms emitted values; tap runs side effects without changing emissions. Interviewers use this to test stream thinking, operator intent, and your ability to avoid subtle production bugs.

blocks.text:
Core idea

If you’re unsure which one to use, ask one question: “Should the next operator receive a different value?”

If yes, use map. If no, and you only need a side effect (logging, loading flags, analytics), use tap.

rows: Run side effects (log, metrics, local state flags)
Can be impure (side effects), but keep intent explicit
Set loading flags, debug, analytics pings
blocks.caption: The practical difference interviewers care about

blocks.text:
Common trap #1

tap does not transform values. Returning a value inside tap is ignored:
Use map when the emitted value must change:

rows: You need a new emitted value
Set isLoading, emit telemetry, debug stream values
Imperative side effects belong outside transformations
Pure data shaping keeps pipeline predictable
blocks.caption: Fast decision guide for day-to-day Angular code

rows: Other developers assume tap is non-transforming; pipeline intent becomes misleading
Hidden shared-state bugs and OnPush surprises
Using tap for critical logic while assuming it always runs
No subscription means pipeline never executes
Ensure stream is consumed (async pipe or explicit subscribe)
blocks.caption: Production footguns to avoid

blocks.text:
Interview summary

map is for transformation; tap is for side effects. A clean stream keeps data-shaping logic in map and keeps tap for observability/imperative work. That boundary is what makes RxJS code readable, testable, and safer in real Angular apps.

seo.title: tap vs map in RxJS: what is the difference?
seo.description: Use map to transform emitted values, and use tap for side effects like logging without changing the stream output.

incidentCard.title: Operator Intent Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate writes `tap(user => ({ ...user, fullName: user.first + ' ' + user.last }))` and expects downstream operators to receive the reshaped object. Another teammate says, "That return value inside `tap` is ignored. If the stream output must change, use `map`; if you are only logging or toggling a flag, use `tap`." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. `map` transforms the emitted value that flows to the next operator. `tap` is for side effects like logging, metrics, or setting local flags, and it passes through the original emission unchanged.
The first explanation is better. `tap` is basically a lighter version of `map`, so returning a new object there is fine when you want to keep the pipeline concise.
The better rule is to keep most RxJS logic in `tap` because it is more flexible, and reserve `map` mainly for obvious collection-style transformations.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key question is whether the next operator should receive a different value. If yes, use `map`. If the value should stay the same and you only need an effect like logging or updating a loading flag, use `tap`.

title: NgRx data flow end-to-end in Angular: actions, reducers, effects, selectors
description: NgRx data flow is a predictable loop, but the high-value explanation is operational: UI intent dispatches actions, reducers stay pure, effects handle async side effects, and selectors expose debug-friendly read models back to Angular components.

blocks.text:
Operational loop

NgRx is most useful when you explain it as a debuggable one-way loop: the UI dispatches intent, reducers compute immutable state, effects run async side effects beside that loop, and selectors expose a clean read model back to the template. That framing is what separates “I know the words” from production understanding.

rows: User clicks/searches; component dispatches an action
Pure function returns next immutable state
Listens to action, calls API, dispatches success/failure
Keeps reducers pure and components thin
Subscribes to selector output and updates UI
Reactive view with minimal manual subscriptions
blocks.caption: NgRx data flow diagram (end-to-end loop)

rows: Reducers must be synchronous and pure
Use memoized selectors for derived view models
Hard to trace intent in DevTools
blocks.caption: What interviewers flag quickly

blocks.text:
Pure reducer update vs effect-driven async update

A quick way to explain NgRx clearly is to separate the sync transition from the async side effect. Reducers update flags synchronously. Effects handle API work and feed the result back into the store.

Compact trace you should be able to say out loud

Load Books click → loadBooks action → reducer sets loading=true → effect calls API → loadBooksSuccess/loadBooksFailure → reducer stores result → selector builds vm → template re-renders. That is the end-to-end loop interviewers want.

Selectors are memoized read models

A selector is not just a getter. It is the read layer that turns raw store state into a reusable, memoized view model so components do not keep sorting, filtering, and combining the same data in multiple places.

Interview summary

In Angular state management with NgRx, components dispatch actions for user intent, reducers compute next immutable state, selectors expose memoized read models, and templates render selector output. Effects handle async side effects and dispatch success/failure actions back into the same loop. If you can explain that loop clearly, you understand NgRx data flow.

seo.title: NgRx data flow in Angular: actions, reducers, effects, selectors, and debug loops
seo.description: Explain NgRx through the real loop: dispatch intent, keep reducers pure, run async work in effects, and expose memoized selectors that make state changes easier to debug.

incidentCard.title: NgRx Flow Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate wants to handle a "Load Books" click by calling the API directly inside the reducer so the reducer can both fetch data and update state in one place. Another teammate says, "That breaks the NgRx flow. The component should dispatch an action, an effect should do the async API work, the reducer should stay pure and build the next state, and selectors should expose the view model back to the component." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. In NgRx, reducers are pure synchronous state transitions. Async work belongs in effects, and selectors are the read layer that derives what the UI needs after state changes.

The first explanation is better. Reducers are the best place for HTTP calls because they already control state updates, so keeping fetch logic there reduces indirection and makes the flow easier to follow.
The better pattern is to keep effects optional and let components call the API directly, then dispatch a final success action only after they already hold the response locally.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The point of NgRx is the predictable loop: components dispatch intent, effects handle async work, reducers compute the next immutable state, and selectors expose derived read models. If reducers start doing HTTP, that separation breaks.

title: NgRx Store vs local component state in Angular: global vs local, and when to use NgRx
description: One of the most practical Angular state management decisions is choosing NgRx Store vs local component state. Use local state for short-lived UI details and use Store for shared, long-lived, globally relevant state. Interviewers expect clear rules for global vs local state in Angular and when NgRx is worth it.

blocks.text:
Core idea

Don’t ask “Is NgRx good or bad?” Ask: “Who needs this state, how long should it live, and does it need traceable transitions?”

If state is screen-local and short-lived, keep it in the component. If it is shared, persistent, or business-critical across features, move it to Store.

Usually local workflow; avoid global complexity too early
Needed across app (header, guards, permissions, API flows)

blocks.caption: What senior reviewers usually call out

blocks.text:
Interview summary

Use local component state for UI-ephemeral, screen-specific concerns. Use NgRx Store for shared, durable, business-relevant state that multiple parts of the app depend on. The best architecture is mixed: local for view mechanics, Store for app-level truth.

seo.title: NgRx store vs component state in Angular: when to use?
seo.description: Use local component state for UI-local short-lived data, and use NgRx Store for shared, long-lived, or cross-feature state.

incidentCard.title: State Ownership Check
incidentCard.scenario: A team wants to put every UI flag into NgRx, including whether a filter drawer is open on one page. Another teammate pushes back and says, "That drawer state is short-lived and local to one screen. NgRx should be reserved for shared, durable, or business-critical state like user session, shared filters, and entity data that multiple parts of the app depend on." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. Local component state is usually the right home for short-lived view details like whether a drawer or modal is open, while NgRx Store is better for shared state that multiple features depend on or that needs a clear global source of truth.
The first explanation is better. The cleanest Angular architecture is to move every piece of state into NgRx so developers never have to decide between local and global ownership.
The better rule is to avoid Store almost entirely and keep nearly everything in components, only introducing NgRx when testing has already become unmanageable.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The useful rule is not "always local" or "always Store." Use local state for screen-specific UI mechanics, and use Store when the state is shared, durable, or important enough that multiple parts of the app need one source of truth.

title: NgRx reducer pure function in practice: immutability, side effects, and common mistakes
description: A reducer in NgRx must be a pure function: same input state + action must always produce the same next state, with no side effects. Interviewers test reducer immutability and whether you can spot forbidden reducer side effects like mutation, Math.random(), HTTP, router navigation, and service calls.

blocks.text:
Core idea

An NgRx reducer is a synchronous, deterministic state transition function. It should only compute the next immutable state from (state, action). If it reaches outside (network, router, random values, services), it is no longer a pure reducer.

blocks.caption: Quick review rubric before merge

blocks.text:
Interview summary

An NgRx reducer pure function does one thing: compute next immutable state. It must avoid mutation and all side effects, including Math.random(), HTTP, router calls, and service usage. Put side effects in effects, keep reducers deterministic, and selector memoization and debugging stay reliable.

seo.title: What is a pure reducer in NgRx?
seo.description: An NgRx reducer is a pure function that returns new state from current state and action without side effects or mutations.

incidentCard.title: Reducer Purity Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate wants to generate an ID with `Math.random()`, call an API, and navigate the router directly inside a reducer so everything happens in one place after `placeOrder`. Another teammate says, "That breaks the reducer contract. Reducers should only compute the next immutable state from the current state and action. IDs, HTTP, routing, and other side effects belong before dispatch or inside effects." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The first explanation is better. Reducers are the best place for follow-up work like HTTP calls and navigation because they already decide how state changes, so centralizing everything there keeps the architecture simpler.
The better rule is to allow mutation inside reducers as long as the code path stays predictable, because purity is mostly about avoiding async behavior, not about object references.

The second explanation is better. A reducer should be a pure, synchronous state transition: same input gives the same output, with no mutation and no side effects. API calls, routing, logging, timestamps, and random IDs belong outside reducers, typically in effects or before dispatch.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key idea is that reducers only compute next state. If logic depends on randomness, time, HTTP, router calls, service calls, or in-place mutation, it no longer behaves like a pure reducer and will break predictability and memoization.

title: NgRx selectors beyond getting state: memoization, derived state, and Angular performance
description: NgRx selectors are not just state getters. They create memoized, composable read models that power derived state in Angular apps. Interviewers expect you to explain selector composition, NgRx selector memoization behavior, and why this improves performance.

blocks.text:
Core idea

Selectors are your app’s read layer. Instead of pushing filtering/sorting/mapping logic into components, you compose selectors once and expose a memoized view model. This keeps components thin and avoids repeated recalculation.

blocks.text:
Interview summary

NgRx selectors are a memoized read-model layer, not just property accessors. Compose selectors from feature state to entities to derived filtered/sorted data, then expose a final view model selector to components. This improves Angular selector performance, reduces component complexity, and keeps state logic reusable.

seo.title: What are NgRx selectors and how does memoization work?
seo.description: NgRx selectors derive and read state efficiently, and memoization reuses the last result until selector inputs change.

incidentCard.title: Selector Memoization Check
incidentCard.scenario: A products page selects the whole root store in the component, then filters, sorts, and rebuilds a new view-model object on every emission. The UI starts recalculating even when unrelated parts of the app change. One teammate says, "Selectors are basically just getters, so doing derivation in the component is fine." Another says, "This logic belongs in composed memoized selectors. Selectors are the read-model layer, and keeping derived state there avoids repeated work and unstable references." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The better rule is to always select the broadest state possible in components so the UI never risks using stale cached data, even if that means rebuilding new arrays and objects on each emission.

The second explanation is better. NgRx selectors are not just property readers; they are a memoized read layer. Putting filter, sort, and view-model shaping into selectors keeps components thinner and avoids unnecessary recalculation when unrelated state changes.

The first explanation is better. Once state comes from the Store, Angular and NgRx already handle enough caching under the hood that doing derived mapping inside the component has no meaningful performance or architecture downside.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The important idea is that selectors are the memoized read-model layer. When you push filtering, sorting, and VM construction into selectors, components stay simpler and Angular avoids extra recalculation when the selector inputs did not actually change.
